Tool Call: retrieve("effect of McRoberts maneuver on obturator nerve")

Retrieved Documents: 32 documents

After reviewing the provided evidence, I confirm that the obturator nerve is indeed the most commonly involved nerve during the McRoberts maneuver due to its anatomical pathway through the obturator canal near the anterior pelvic wall. Compression occurs because of increased intra-pelvic pressure from hyperflexion, leading to referred pain or dysfunction typical of obturator nerve entrapment. No contradictory findings were presented regarding another nerve being preferentially affected in this scenario.
\boxed{B}
Based on the retrieved evidence, I agree with the original conclusion. The McRoberts maneuver increases pressure on the obturator nerve due to its passage through the obturator canal near the anterior pelvic wall, where mechanical stress from hyperflexion leads to compression — supported by clinical reports linking this maneuver to obturator nerve irritation and confirmed by documentation of obturator nerve blocks used specifically to manage reflexes during obstetric procedures.
